facts_disclosed: Nai Nai speaks only Cantonese, very close

turns.content: random question but do you know if theres a good way to record someone telling stories... like audio recording and then getting it transcribed and maybe translated

Great question! There are a few good options depending on what you need:

- **For recording:** Your phone's built-in voice recorder works fine, or apps like Otter.ai or Rev have better quality. A quiet room makes a huge difference.
- **For transcription + translation:** It depends on the language. If it's a widely spoken language, automated tools can handle it. For more nuanced work, a professional service might be better.

What language are the stories in, and what's the context? That'll help me point you to the right tool.

cantonese. its my nai nai... my grandma. she only speaks cantonese and she tells these incredible stories about growing up in guangzhou but i can only understand like half of what shes saying and i feel like i need to capture them properly before... you know. shes getting older

Oh, that's such a meaningful project. And I totally understand the urgency — those stories are irreplaceable.

For Cantonese specifically, here are your best options:

1. **Recording:** Use your phone but get a decent setup — sit close to her, minimize background noise. Record in longer sessions (30-60 min) so she can talk naturally without feeling rushed.

2. **Transcription/Translation:** This is trickier with Cantonese because most AI tools are trained on Mandarin. Your options:
   - **Google Translate** has Cantonese speech recognition but it's hit or miss, especially with older dialects
   - **Professional transcription services** that handle Cantonese (places like GoTranscript or Rev have human translators)
   - **Community option** — are there any Cantonese-speaking family members or friends who could help translate the recordings?

3. **Whisper by OpenAI** can do Cantonese transcription and it's free, though accuracy varies with accent and dialect.

How much Cantonese do you speak yourself? Even partial understanding helps a lot with checking translations.

my cantonese is genuinely embarrassing lol. like i can do basic conversation stuff and food words obviously but when nai nai gets into the real stories — the details about her neighbourhood and the people she knew and what guangzhou was like back then — i lose the thread. i get the emotion of it though? like i can tell which parts make her happy and which parts are sad even when i dont catch every word

That emotional understanding is actually really valuable — it means you have genuine connection even across the language gap. And honestly, a lot of people in bilingual families have that experience where they can feel a conversation even when the vocabulary gets too advanced.

Here's what I'd suggest as a practical approach:

1. **Record everything.** Even if you can't understand it all now, the recordings are the archive. Use your phone, hit record, and just let her talk.

2. **Take notes on what you DO understand.** After each session, jot down the bits you caught — names, places, the emotional beats. That creates a rough map of each story.

3. **Get translations done in batches.** Once you have several recordings, find a Cantonese speaker (ideally someone older who understands the dialect and cultural references) to translate them properly.

4. **Ask her simple questions to guide the storytelling.** Even in your basic Cantonese — "Tell me about your school," "What was the food like?" Simple prompts can unlock incredible stories.

Does she live nearby? How often do you get to see her?

she lives with my parents so i see her whenever i go home which is most weekends. we're really close honestly she practically raised me because mum and dad were always working. she used to walk me to school and pick me up every day and we'd go to the park and she'd tell me about guangzhou and i didnt even realise half of it was going over my head because i was just happy being with her

That's really beautiful. The fact that she was the one who was there every day — walking you to school, taking you to the park — that's a huge bond. And those park conversations were probably laying the foundation for the stories she tells now, even if you didn't catch every word as a kid.

Since you see her most weekends, you're actually in a great position to do this gradually. You don't need to make it a big formal "oral history project" — just:

- Start recording casually during your normal visits. "Nai Nai, tell me that story again about..." and let her go.
- Even 15-20 minutes per visit adds up fast. Over a few months you'd have hours of material.
- The informal setting will probably get better stories anyway — she'll be more relaxed and natural.

Have you ever asked your parents to help translate? Since they presumably speak fluent Cantonese.

yeah mum is fluent obviously but she's always so busy with the restaurant that getting her to sit down and listen to recordings feels like asking a lot. my dad is the same. i might ask my cousin actually she speaks way better cantonese than me and shes less busy. i just... i reckon i need to start doing this soon instead of just thinking about it. nai nai is 83 and shes still sharp as anything but 83 is 83 you know

You're absolutely right — the best time to start is now. And 83 and sharp is a gift, so take advantage of it while she's able to tell the stories the way she wants to tell them.

Your cousin sounds like a great option for translation help. Here's a simple plan to get started this weekend:

1. **Download a voice recording app** (or just use your phone's built-in one)
2. **This weekend, just record one conversation.** Don't overthink it. Ask her one question about Guangzhou and let her talk.
3. **Send the recording to your cousin** and ask her to summarize the key points, even if she doesn't do a full word-for-word translation right away.

Once you have that first recording done, the momentum builds naturally. The hardest part is literally just pressing record for the first time.

Would you want to eventually compile these into something — like a written family history, or just keep them as audio archives?
i hadnt thought that far ahead honestly but a written family history would be so cool. even just like a document with the stories and maybe some of her old photos

That would be an incredible keepsake — stories paired with photos, maybe organized by theme or time period. You could even make copies for other family members. Something like that becomes more valuable with every year that passes.

Do you know if she has many photos from Guangzhou, or mostly from after she came to Australia?

she has a few really old ones from guangzhou that she keeps in this tin box in her room... theyre so faded but you can still make things out. mostly shes in them as a teenager. the australia ones start from when my parents were kids. i should scan those too honestly before they deteriorate more

Oh definitely scan those — old photos in a tin box are living on borrowed time. You can use a phone scanning app like Google PhotoScan to digitize them pretty quickly without needing a flatbed scanner. It corrects for glare and everything.

That could actually be part of your recording sessions too. Pull out the photos, ask her to tell you who's in them and what was happening. Photos are incredible memory triggers — she'll probably remember details she wouldn't think to mention otherwise.

oh thats such a good idea using the photos as prompts!! ok im genuinely excited about this now. im gonna do it this weekend no cap. just gonna put my phone on the table and ask her to tell me about her favourite place in guangzhou. thank you for talking me into actually doing this instead of just feeling guilty about not doing it lol

Yes! That's a perfect first prompt — favourite place in Guangzhou. She'll light up, guaranteed.

And don't feel guilty about not starting sooner. You're starting now, that's what matters. A lot of people never get around to it at all. The fact that you want to preserve her stories says a lot about how much she means to you.

Good luck this weekend. I hope she tells you something amazing.
